Title: China says banned 46 US entities from public procurement markets
Article:
BEIJING: China said Monday it had banned public agencies from buying products from 46 US entities, including Lockheed Martin, Raytheon and Boeing's defence division.
Companies with US investments in China are excluded, according to a statement from China's finance ministry, which said measures would take effect from Monday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Titled land seized': Gua Musang settlers cry foul as farmland declared forest reserve
Article:
GUA MUSANG: More than 100 settlers at the Kesedar Chalil Land Development Scheme here are in limbo after learning that the land they have cultivated for nearly 20 years has been classified as a forest reserve and seized.
The affected residents, some of whom already hold land titles, claimed they were previously led to understand the plot was state land earmarked for cultivation and legitimate ownership.
Two private companies had also established commercial plantations in the area and have harvested crops multiple times.
Chalil Federal Village Development and Security Committee chairman Hasan Saleh said the community was devastated when the Forestry Department seized the land last Monday.
He said that residents first began clearing and cultivating the plots in 2008, planting oil palm and rubber trees that have since become the primary livelihood for local families.
"We were told the area was seized because it falls within a forest reserve.
"Yet, when we conducted checks with the Gua Musang District and Land Office back in 2008, it was classified as state land, and we were told titles could be issued upon paying the land premium," Hasan said.
"To date, 122 residents and two companies are affected by this issue, and a number of them already hold valid land titles.
"As far as we understand, the process of securing a land grant is stringent and requires multi-agency approvals, so it makes no sense how titles could have been mistakenly issued inside a forest reserve.
"We are calling for an investigation into this matter because it involves the sweat and tears of residents who have tilled this land for nearly two decades," he added.
Hanipah Tahir, 65, said she was shocked to find her rubber plantation seized last Monday without any prior warning or notice.
The 4-ha plot has been officially titled for over a decade and serves as her family's sole source of income.
"I am utterly baffled by how land that has carried a legal title for over a decade can suddenly be declared part of a forest reserve, and now we are being told our grants will be revoked," she said.
"The real question here is: if it truly was a forest reserve all along, why were our land titles approved in the first place, and why did so many residents pay their land premiums to the government?"
Former Paloh assemblyman Amran Arifin also attended a solidarity meeting with the affected residents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran FM Araghchi signals 'major breakthrough' in US talks
Article:
BURGENSTOCK, Switzerland: Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Monday there was "major progress" in talks with the United States.
"Pakistani and Qatari mediation has delivered major progress to end Lebanon War," Araghchi posted on X.
"Oil and petrochem exports are waived, blockade lifted, some frozen assets released, and major reconstruction & development plan launched for Iran."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Online threats, insults fuel South Africa's anti-foreigner hate
Article:
INFLAMMATORY social media posts – including of men brandishing a machete or calling foreigners "leeches" – are stoking emotions around an unofficial demand for illegal immigrants to leave South Africa by the end of the month, fuelling a volatile situation, analysts say.
Thousands of foreign nationals – including from Ghana, Mozambique, Nigeria and Malawi – have already headed for the borders as fringe anti-illegal immigration groups push their June 30 "deadline."
The ultimatum has no legal backing but has gained traction through countrywide protests, threats at places where foreigners live or work, and a toxic social media campaign that analysts say has been building for years.
"Every morning, when you wake up, you see a traumatising video telling people that they're going to kill people before June 30," said Tino Maclean, an activist helping Zimbabweans to leave.
"You know the impact of social media these days: when people say they're going to kill you, you can't sleep," he told AFP.
Social media has also been effective in rallying South Africans behind radical citizen-led groups mobilising against undocumented migrants, experts said.
"The best disinformation campaign is to convince a few people that thousands are convinced," said Aldu Cornelissen from Murmur Intelligence, which analyses online content.
The company has found that a relatively small number of highly active accounts, influencer communities and alternative media networks generate and amplify a disproportionate share of the anti-immigrant content.
Posts have included videos with captions reading "June 30, I can't wait," or images of the date pierced by bullet holes.
The inflammatory rhetoric has been accompanied by a wave of disinformation.
AFP Fact Check has debunked numerous videos falsely presented as evidence of attacks on foreign nationals that were in fact filmed years earlier or in other countries.
Other posts falsely claimed to show an official government announcement of the June 30 "deadline" using AI-generated notices bearing the national coat of arms.
"It is very clear online that there are groups and organisations and individuals who are happy to light a match on a very volatile situation and then walk away when that fire erupts," public works minister Dean Macpherson told journalists Friday, calling on police to act.
Police spokeswoman Athlenda Mathe told AFP that intelligence officers were monitoring social media and engaging platforms where necessary.
TikTok last week banned the account of one of the movement's most vocal leaders, Jacinta Ngobese-Zuma, which had more than 378,000 followers, but her other social media accounts remained active.
[[nid:1469403]]
Murmur Intelligence says today's anti-foreigner mobilisation reflects an ecosystem that has been developing for years, transforming emotionally charged incidents into broader narratives that blame migrants for crime, unemployment and other state failures.
"These interests converge around xenophobic civil unrest," co-founder Kyle Findlay said at a Johannesburg event against hate speech this week.
The roots included 2020 campaigns by #PutSouthAfricaFirst campaign and the radical Operation Dudula group that evolved into a "self-sustaining ecosystem" fuelled by anonymous media channels, political support and coordinated online mobilisation, he said.
South Africa's "modern xenophobic movement has been built intentionally over the past six years" but repeated warnings have "fallen on deaf ears", Findlay said.
This "movement has been working to make the forest drier and drier and to raise the grievances that can turn into the sparks to light the forest fire," he said.
South Africa's laws prohibiting hate speech and incitement to violence mostly predate modern social media platforms, said Kimal Harvey, an attorney at the Legal Resources Centre.
The challenge is "translating the South African legal system to the online space", he told AFP.
In 2023, a former Operation Dudula activist was issued a fine or jail term for circulating an inflammatory anti-foreigner voice note on WhatsApp.
Duduzile Zuma-Sambudla, daughter of former president Jacob Zuma, is on trial for incitement over social media posts following her father's jailing in July 2021 which sparked unrest that claimed more than 350 lives.
Experts say algorithms continue to reward emotionally charged content to the profit of the companies running social media platforms.
"They are not going to self-regulate. It is too lucrative," said Sharon Ekambaram, who heads the Refugee and Migrant Rights Programme at Lawyers for Human Rights.
"Algorithms decide what we get to see first," said Phathiswa Magopeni, chair of Unesco's Social Media 4 Peace coalition.
"This is why outrage outperforms accuracy."
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia opens lower on cautious tone
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened lower on Monday on a cautious tone after a flat performance last Friday, amid Wall Street and Hong Kong market closures due to a public holiday.
At 9.10am, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) shed 2.75 points to 1,709.28 from Friday's close of 1,712.03, after opening 1.56 points easier at 1,710.47.
Market breadth was negative, with decliners outpacing gainers 206 to 189.
A total of 343 counters were unchanged, while 1,980 were untraded and 14 suspended.
Turnover amounted to 228.65 million shares worth RM118.83 million.
Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice-president of equity research Thong Pak Leng told Bernama that despite the market closure, the delay in a US-Iran accord and Israel's latest strikes on Lebanon have heightened uncertainty surrounding peace talks.
As a result, Brent crude rose above the US$80 per barrel level.
On the domestic front, he said the FBM KLCI's lower close at the 1,700 level on Friday indicated that bargain hunters remained active, with buying interest focused predominantly on bluechips.
Among heavyweights, PPB Group was down by 44 sen to RM9.68, IOI Corporation fell six sen to RM4.27, Petronas Dagangan eased 18 sen to RM18.56, Maxis slid three sen to RM3.39, and Maybank was eight sen lower at RM11.10.
Of the most active stocks, Dagang Nexchange added half-a-sen to 47 sen, AirAsia X was five sen weaker at RM1.28, Widad Group and Tanco were flat each at two sen and 14 sen, respectively, and Capital A declined 1.5 sen to 42.5 sen.
Among top gainers, F&N rose 30 sen to RM28.38, Concrete Engineering Products (Cepco) gained 26 sen to RM3.97, Skyechip increased 11 sen to RM3.20, Petronas Chemicals Group ticked up nine sen to RM,4.58, and Hong Leong Bank was eight sen firmer at RM22.24.
The top losers were led by PPB Group, followed by Malaysian Pacific Industries, which erased 32 sen to RM49.58, Nestle was down by 24 sen to RM95.98, Petronas Dagangan eased 18 sen to RM18.56, and KLCC Prop & REITS-Stapled Sec (KLCC) was 14 sen easier at RM9.38.
On the index board, the FBM Emas Index declined 22.29 points to 12,658.76, while the FBM Top 100 Index weakened 23.35 points to 12,496.27 and the FBM Emas Shariah Index dropped 19.67 points to 12,538.99.
The FBM Mid 70 Index decreased 47.67 points to 18,114.91, while the FBM ACE Index shed 6.46 points to 4,786.06.
By sector, the Financial Services Index fell 52.75 points to 20,151.83, and the Plantation Index trimmed 8.05 points to 8,865.17, but the Industrial Products and Services Index rose 0.09 of a point to 190.66, and the Energy Index added 1.67 points to 767.94.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan's Nikkei rises past 72,000 mark for first time as AI euphoria persists
Article:
TOKYO: Japan's Nikkei share gauge surged past the 72,000 mark for the first time on Monday as euphoria over AI investment outweighed uncertainty over US-Iran peace talks.
The benchmark Nikkei 225 advanced 1.4 per cent to 72,247.21 in early trading after touching a record intraday high of 72,269.64. The broader Topix gained 1.1 per cent to 4,089.59.
The government of Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi plans to set a target of about 370 trillion yen (US$2.29 trillion) in public and private investment in strategic sectors including AI and chips by 2040, the Nikkei reported on Friday.
Shares extended gains after Qatar and Pakistan, mediators to peace talks between the US and Iran, said progress had been made and negotiations would continue following a tense opening.
"AI-related companies are once again playing a leading role in driving the market," said Wataru Akiyama, an equities strategist at Nomura Securities. "A high level of vigilance regarding developments in the situation in Iran and the Middle East is likely to persist."
Nonferrous metals led Topix sector gains, up 7.57 per cent, followed by electric appliances and glass & ceramics products, which rose 2.08 per cent and 2.05 per cent respectively. Real estate shares were among the laggards, down 1.07 per cent.
There were 146 advancers in the Nikkei 225 against 75 decliners and three unchanged.
J.Front Retailing led gainers with a 16.24 per cent surge after activist investor 3D Investment Partners said it took a 5.10 per cent stake in the department store operator.
It was followed by Yaskawa Electric, trading up 9.02 per cent, and robot maker Fanuc, which gained 8.10 per cent.
The largest losers were Taiyo Yuden, down 3.37 per cent, followed by Tokyo Electric Power, 3.05 per cent lower, and Obayashi, which lost 2.99 per cent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RHB Research keeps 'Buy' call on VS Industry, lowers target price on weaker earnings
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: VS Industry Bhd is expected to stage a gradual earnings recovery from the fourth quarter of financial year 2026 (Q4 FY26) onwards, according to RHB Research.
In a note, the firm said this is supported by improving order flows from key customers, stronger seasonal demand and the ramp-up of new programmes.
"The Philippines facility should move closer to a breakeven point in FY27 as production volumes increase, further supported by the newly awarded programme commencing in the second half of 2026.
"With major customer cost-down exercises largely completed, we expect utilisation rates to improve progressively, allowing new programme contributions to gain traction and support a gradual earnings recovery into FY27.
"VS Industry's healthy balance sheet positions it well to capture market share amidst a period of industry consolidation," it said.
Despite maintaining its "Buy" call, RHB Research lowered its target price on the stock to 31 sen from 45 sen following weaker-than-expected results and earnings forecast revisions.
Downside risks to the recommendation include weaker-than-expected global consumer demand and the potential loss of key customers, it said.
Meanwhile, the firm also said VS Insustry's nine-month FY26 results came in below expectations, as the group posted a core net loss of RM31.9 million.
This was due to weaker orders from key customers, soft end-market demand and margin compression arising from low utilisation rates.
Start-up losses from its Philippines operation also remained elevated, although these narrowed sequentially in the third quarter.
Following the results, RHB Research now expects VS Industry to record a core net loss of RM12.6 million for FY26, compared with its earlier forecast of a RM26.6 million core net profit.
The firm also cut its FY27 and FY28 earnings forecasts by 30 per cent and 32 per cent, respectively, after lowering its medium-term sales and margin assumptions.
Nevertheless, RHB Research said the group's valuation remains attractive, with the stock trading well below its five-year average despite expectations of a medium-term earnings recovery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gold rebounds from one-week low as Iran cites progress in peace talks
Article:
BENGALURU: Gold rose over one per cent on Monday, rebounding from a more than one-week low hit in the previous session, as Brent crude oil prices fell after Iran claimed progress in US-Iran peace talks, easing concerns around inflation and higher interest rates.
FUNDAMENTALS
Spot gold was up 1.2 per cent at US$4,209.03 per ounce, as of 0112 GMT. US gold futures for August delivery fell 0.5 per cent to US$4,225.80.
An Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson said good progress has been made during the quadrilateral talks in Switzerland, according to Iran's Press TV.
Iran-US peace talks in Switzerland stretched into their second day on Monday, after a tense opening marked by Tehran's announcement that it had again closed the Strait of Hormuz and US President Donald Trump repeating his threats to resume attacks on Iran.
Brent crude futures fell 0.5 per cent after Iran claimed progress in peace talks, easing fears of elevated inflation and higher-for-longer global interest rates.
Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh's emphasis on inflation in last week's press conference, without more nuanced commentary about what might clear the bar for a rate hike, led investors to conclude an increase was coming soon and begin bidding up bond yields.
Most global brokerages are betting on the Fed to hold interest rates steady for the rest of 2026, reversing from expectations of two interest rate cuts at the start of the year, as policymakers navigate elevated inflation risks and a resilient labour market.
Gold demand was modest in India last week as prices fell to their lowest level in two-and-a-half months and remained volatile, while top consumer China flipped to a discount.
Swiss gold exports fell nine per cent in May from the previous month as lower shipments to India and Hong Kong offset higher deliveries to Britain and China, Swiss customs data showed.
Spot silver rose 2.6 per cent to US$66.60 per ounce, platinum gained 1.3 per cent to US$1,684.85, and palladium was up 1.5 per cent at US$1,276.88.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Supply restocking may support crude above pre-conflict levels: CIMB Securities
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Inventory rebuilding by countries following recent supply disruptions could underpin global oil demand and keep crude prices above pre-conflict levels, according to CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd.
The firm said Brent crude prices of US$75 to US$80 per barrel suggest the market is pricing in a relatively smooth normalisation of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
The impact of supply disruptions has so far been cushioned by lower global refinery utilisation rates, alternative export routes used by Gulf producers to bypass the strait, and drawdowns from strategic petroleum reserves.
CIMB Securities maintained its Brent crude price forecasts of US$85 per barrel for the second half of 2026 and US$80 per barrel for the first half of 2027.
The firm said risks to its outlook remain on both the demand and supply sides.
On the demand front, CIMB Securities said weaker-than-expected oil consumption in China could weigh on prices.
On the supply side, lower inventory levels in China, a slower recovery in Persian Gulf oil production and delays in restoring normal oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz could push prices higher.
Even under its base-case assumption that the strait reopens in the near term, CIMB Securities estimates cumulative supply losses during the conflict exceeded one billion barrels.
It said replenishing inventories and rebuilding supply buffers could take a year or longer, supporting crude demand and helping to keep Brent prices above US$70 per barrel.
"This remains a healthy price level to sustain upstream investment activity," it added.
CIMB Securities maintained its "Overweight" call on the oil and gas sector, citing the potential for a renewed upcycle in upstream capital expenditure (capex) spending and attractive valuations among oilfield services companies.
It said improved project economics, supported by stronger and more sustainable oil prices, and the risk of missing the current investment cycle if projects are delayed are among the key catalysts for higher investment spending by Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) and global oil majors.
The firm added that the completion of Petronas' workforce restructuring exercise, which began in 2025, could also support a recovery in investment activity.
While Petronas faces near-term constraints from cash commitments related to the acquisition of Saudi Aramco's 50 per cent stake in Pengerang Refining Company and Pengerang Petrochemical Company (PRefChem), as well as potentially higher government dividend requirements, CIMB Securities believes these headwinds are temporary.
The firm said stronger cash flows from elevated oil prices and Petronas' substantial cash reserves should help offset the impact.
Among upstream service providers, CIMB Securities named Dayang Enterprise Holdings Bhd as its preferred pick, citing an attractive risk-reward profile and the potential for earnings upgrades.
The firm said the stock is trading at 10 times price-to-earnings, below its five-year average of 12 times.
Other beneficiaries of a recovery in upstream capex spending include Velesto Energy Bhd, as well as offshore support vessel operators Keyfield International Bhd and Perdana Petroleum Bhd.
CIMB Securities maintained its "Buy" calls on Dialog Group Bhd and MISC Bhd, citing strong project pipelines that could support future earnings growth.
It said key catalysts for Dialog include the financial close of the Pengerang Energy Complex's aromatics plant, while MISC is well positioned to benefit from a renewed investment cycle in upstream production assets.
Overall, key risks to the firm's "Overweight" call include less severe-than-expected supply disruptions from the West Asia conflict, a sharper-than-expected global economic slowdown and weaker-than-expected growth in global oil demand.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran-US agree on 60-day roadmap to end war
Article:
BURGENSTOCK, Switzerland: Iran and the United States agreed on a roadmap towards reaching a final deal to end the war within 60 days, mediators Pakistan and Qatar said in a joint statement on Monday.
"The High Level Committee has agreed upon a roadmap towards reaching a final deal within 60 days, laying the foundation for the immediate commencement of further technical talks," the joint statement read.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor state election:PM Anwar to announce list of PH candidates tonight
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Pakatan Harapan (PH) chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim is scheduled to announce the full list of 56 candidates for the coalition for the 16th Johor State Election.
The candidate announcement ceremony is scheduled to take place at 8pm tonight at Bukit Gambir Extreme Park field, thus completing the entire list of PH candidates that will be contested in the election.
PH's top leadership, including DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke and Amanah president Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu, are among those scheduled to be present tonight, in addition to the coalition's machinery and supporters.
So far, PH through DAP has announced five candidates, namely lawyer Chu Poh Yee (Mengkibol), private secretary to the Deputy Finance Minister Nor Zulaila Abd Ghani (Tiram), Johor DAP Socialist Youth (DAPSY) chief Lee Wern Yiing (Johor Jaya), former incumbent Wong Bor Yang (Senai) and special assistant to Kulai Member of Parliament Mohamad Shafwan Ani (Bukit) Permai.
PKR has announced Arthur Chiong Sen Sern who will defend the Bukit Batu state seat.
In a post on social media yesterday, Anwar said the state deserves a leadership that is integrity-based, credible and works hard for the progress of the state and the well-being of the people.
The Election Commission (EC) has set June 27 as nomination day, July 7 as early voting day and July 11 as polling day. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dollar firms as cracks emerge in peace deal, pound dips on Starmer uncertainty
Article:
SINGAPORE: The dollar was firm on Monday as uncertainty clouded a tentative US-Iran peace deal following threats from President Donald Trump to restart the war in the Middle East and Tehran's announcement that it had closed the Strait of Hormuz.
Despite rising tensions, US-Iran peace talks stretched into their second day in Switzerland under the terms of a memorandum of understanding reached last week to extend a ceasefire from April for at least another 60 days.
Chris Weston, head of research at Pepperstone, said it was not surprising how quickly adherence to the terms of the deal had broken down.
"Ultimately, what matters to markets is the flow of cargo through the Strait of Hormuz."
Shipping data showed the number of ships that passed through the waterway fell sharply on Sunday after Tehran said it had closed the strait. That lifted oil prices, with Brent crude futures climbing 1.30 per cent to US$81.62 a barrel.
"The physical market remains tight and that should provide some support, but flows in FX and commodities, particularly gold, will continue to be heavily influenced by developments in the energy complex," Weston said.
Sterling eased in early trading as traders assessed the political tumult in Britain, where Prime Minister Keir Starmer was considering his political future after rival Andy Burnham's decisive election victory to parliament.
The pound was 0.24 per cent weaker at US$1.32055, while the euro softened 0.1 per cent to US$1.1462. The Australian dollar was last down 0.19 per cent at US$0.70035, while the New Zealand dollar last bought US$0.573.
Markets will be focused on Burnham's views on fiscal policy and whether there will be any relaxation of the current fiscal rules, Commonwealth Bank of Australia strategists said.
"A loosening in fiscal rules would likely be poorly received by the UK bond market and weigh on the pound," they said in a note.
The Japanese yen slipped to 161.53 per dollar, hovering near a two-year low reached last week. A break beyond 161.96 would take the yen to its weakest level since 1986.
Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Monday that authorities were prepared to respond appropriately to currency moves at any time, reiterating their previous stance.
"The MOF may be getting sore necks watching USD/JPY surge into the 2024 high," said Matt Simpson, senior market analyst at StoneX. "Yet they may also feel powerless to do anything about it, as intervening against the tide of a hawkish Fed and strong US fundamentals could prove costly and futile."
The yen has erased gains made after a round of interventions from April 30, as a hawkish tilt by the Federal Reserve has led traders to ramp up bets on rate increases this year.
Treasuries remained under pressure on Monday, with yields on two-year notes rising to their highest level since early 2025 at 4.2276 per cent.
Traders are anticipating 43 basis points of hikes this year, with a 25-basis-point increase fully priced in by September.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stocks slip in Asia, oil up on peace doubts
Article:
SYDNEY: Most share markets slipped in Asia on Monday as doubts about the Middle East peace process sent oil prices and bond yields up again, leading investors to price in more risk of higher US interest rates.
Sterling eased amid reports Prime Minister Keir Starmer was considering his political future, after rival Andy Burnham's decisive election victory to parliament prompted more ministers in the governing Labour Party to call for him to go.
US President Donald Trump posted that Starmer was set to resign, while also threatening fresh attacks on Iran even as Vice President JD Vance met Iranian officials for the first talks under an interim peace deal.
The talks were overshadowed by Tehran's announcement that it had again closed the Strait of Hormuz, with tracking sites showing fewer vessels transiting after 32 ships made the passage on Friday and 26 on Saturday.
Iran's threats were enough to push Brent crude futures up 1.1 per cent to US$81.43 a barrel, still far below its May peak of US$126.41. US crude firmed 2.7 per cent to US$78.70 a barrel, but remained above the US$67 level at which it traded before the war began.
S&P 500 futures eased 0.5 per cent, while Nasdaq futures lost 0.7 per cent. In Europe, EUROSTOXX 50 futures fell 0.5 per cent, while DAX futures dropped 0.3 per cent and FTSE futures dipped 0.1 per cent.
Japan's Nikkei edged up 0.7 per cent, having climbed almost eight per cent last week to all-time highs. South Korea's red-hot market fell 0.9 per cent, after surging more than 11 per cent last week on demand for semiconductor stocks.
MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan eased 0.4 per cent.
MARKET NARROWS ODDS ON FED HIKE
Treasuries remained under pressure following a hawkish turn by the Federal Reserve last week that led markets to price in a 75 per cent chance of a rate hike as early as September.
Futures imply 38 basis points of tightening by year-end, while yields on two-year notes rose 4 basis points to their highest level since early 2025 at 4.2276 per cent.
"Our baseline call is for patience and a first hike in the second half of 2027, but believe the margin for error and the tolerance for further inflation is limited, with genuine risks of earlier hikes," said Fabio Bassi, head of cross-asset strategy at JPMorgan.
"We remain constructive on risk assets as improving labour markets will keep rates higher for longer, supporting a narrow leadership in Quality Growth, Large Cap and Tech," he added. "We see upside risks for the S&P target tilted towards 8,000."
The Fed's favoured gauge of core inflation is due on Thursday and is forecast to rise a tick to 3.4 per cent in May, underlining the risk of tighter policy.
Central bank speakers include Governor Christopher Waller and Federal Reserve Bank of New York President John Williams.
The Fed's hawkish outlook kept the dollar supported at 161.44 yen, with only the threat of Japanese intervention preventing a test of resistance at 161.96, a peak from mid-2024.
The euro eased to US$1.1462, after hitting a three-month low on Friday at US$1.1418. Political uncertainty nudged sterling down 0.2 per cent to US$1.3210.
"Amid the uncertainty around a potential challenge against the UK PM and what that means for the fiscal outlook, the likelihood is that gilts will remain under selling pressure to start the week," said Skye Masters, head of market research at NAB.
In commodity markets, higher bond yields weighed on non-interest-paying gold, which slipped 0.1 per cent to US$4,154 an ounce.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Netizens stunned by woman changing bulb atop Petronas Twin Towers [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A viral video showing maintenance workers carrying out repairs near the pinnacle of one of the Petronas Twin Towers has left social media users both amazed and terrified by the dizzying heights involved.
Posted on TikTok by user Siva wwc, the footage shows a team of workers secured with safety harnesses as they perform maintenance work on the uppermost section of one of the iconic towers.
Adding to the dramatic scene was a thick blanket of fog and clouds surrounding the structure, creating the impression that the workers were operating above the clouds.
One of the most talked-about moments in the video features a female worker wearing a red headscarf and full safety gear emerging from the highest accessible section of the tower before carefully making her way onto the exterior structure to replace a fused light bulb.
Despite the staggering height, she appeared remarkably calm and was seen waving at the videographer before getting to work.
Another maintenance worker could also be seen nearby carrying out inspection and maintenance duties while attached to multiple safety lines.
The video quickly drew widespread attention, with many viewers expressing admiration for the workers responsible for maintaining one of Malaysia's most recognisable landmarks.
@muhammadfirdausrohani8
.
bunyi asal  - firdaus88
Among the most common reactions were questions about the salaries earned by those performing such high-altitude work.
"How much is the pay for this kind of job?" one user asked.
Another wrote: "Even if they paid me RM5,000 a day, I still wouldn't go out there."
Others praised the workers' professionalism and courage, with one commenter describing them as the "unsung heroes who keep our landmarks shining".
The Petronas Twin Towers, which stand at 452m, remain among the world's most recognisable skyscrapers and held the title of the world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hizbollah rejects Israeli 'security zone' in southern Lebanon
Article:
BEIRUT: Hizbollah chief Naim Qassem on Sunday rejected any Israeli security zone in Lebanon, after Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu vowed his troops would remain in the country's south for "as long as necessary."
Israeli troops "remaining on Lebanese land is impossible. There are no security zones for Israel... we have a national army which deploys, and it is responsible for preserving sovereignty, and it is who we cooperate with," Qassem said in a televised address.
"Israel is an aggressor and must leave. America bears full responsibility," Qassem said, adding that "Israel will not remain in Lebanon, even if it increases its crimes, and we will defend ourselves."
Israeli forces who invaded south Lebanon after war erupted on March 2 are operating inside a so-called "security zone" around a dozen kilometres deep inside Lebanese territory along the border.
Netanyahu said Sunday that "we will remain in the security zone in southern Lebanon for as long as necessary to protect the cherished residents of the north and all the citizens of Israel... Nothing will alter that commitment."
Israel's defence minister meanwhile said that Israeli forces had standing orders to act against any threat they encountered inside Lebanon.
Qassem's address came as Washington and Teheran held talks in Switzerland after this week signing a preliminary agreement to end the broader Middle East war, which includes a halt to the hostilities in Lebanon.
Ongoing Israeli strikes in recent days have threatened to derail the deal, but fighting in Lebanon has paused since Saturday evening, after Iran again closed the strategic Strait of Hormuz in response to Israel's attacks on Lebanon.
"Any ceasefire under the banner of a comprehensive cessation of hostilities – we have already committed to this if it happens, but we will not accept any violation. We will confront any violation... we will deal with it as we see fit," Qassem said.
He urged Lebanese authorities to "take advantage of the path of the memorandum of understanding."
"Go and sort out your situation with Iran. America is sorting out its situation with Iran, all the Arab states are sorting out their situation with Iran.... why are you still standing still?" he added.
"Look at the great Iran, closing the Strait of Hormuz for the sake of Lebanon. This is a weapon in your hands," he said, addressing the Lebanese authorities, adding, "Take up this weapon and use it."
Under US pressure, Lebanon in April began direct talks with Israel in Washington aimed at ending the hostilities and separating the Israel-Hizbollah conflict – which erupted with Hizbollah's rocket fire at Israel in support of its backer Iran – from the regional war.
Hizbollah has firmly rejected the direct talks, a fifth round of which is due to begin next week.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil rises after Iran shuts Hormuz again, Trump threatens new attacks
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil prices rose on Monday after shipping through the Strait of Hormuz slowed while talks between US and Iranian officials in their first meeting under an interim peace deal got off to a bumpy start.
Brent crude futures climbed 54 cents, or 0.67 per cent, to US$81.11 a barrel by 0030 GMT, after touching a high of US$82.30 at the start of trading.
US West Texas Intermediate crude futures were at US$78.62 a barrel, up US$2.02, or 2.64 per cent, ahead of the contract's expiry later on Monday. The more active August contract rose US$1.43 to US$77.28 a barrel.
There was no settlement in the US market on Friday due to a holiday.
The number of ships that passed through the Strait of Hormuz fell sharply on Sunday after Iran announced it had again closed the waterway, citing Israeli and US violations of the interim peace deal, shipping data showed.
"The market's expectation of the opening of the Strait has been premature," MST Marquee head of energy research Saul Kavonic said.
"Iran is likely to continue to find pretexts to stymie flows through the Strait, as that remains their only point of leverage into the mid-terms, which they are unlikely to let go of."
US President Donald Trump threatened to resume attacks on Iran even as US Vice President JD Vance met Iranian officials on Sunday for the first talks under an interim peace deal, while Tehran said the US had failed to meet its commitment to halt fighting in Lebanon.
Israeli strikes in Lebanon killed at least 20 people on Saturday, Lebanon's state news agency NNA said, one day after a ceasefire with Hezbollah took effect, aimed at halting months of escalating violence.
"The situation in Lebanon continues to pose a serious ongoing threat to both the ceasefire and the reopening of the Strait," IG market analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note.
Still, oil prices fell more than eight per cent last week on expectations of more supply from the release of cargoes stranded inside the Gulf and the potential lifting of US sanctions on Iranian oil as part of the US-Iran deal.
More than 25 million barrels of Iranian oil have passed through the virtual blockade line since Monday, the head of the National Iranian Oil Company, Hamid Bovard, told state TV on Sunday.
The United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Iraq have offered more oil to customers in the past week.
Iraq plans to restore crude oil production gradually to between 4.2 million and 4.3 million barrels per day, Iraq's deputy oil minister for upstream affairs said in a statement on Sunday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel kills two near West Bank settlement
Article:
JERUSALEM: Israel's military said Sunday that they had killed two people who threw Molotov cocktails near the West Bank settlement of Karmei Tzur.
Soldiers moved against a group of people burning tyres and throwing petrol bombs towards the settlement, the military said in a statement.
They killed two and neutralised a third person, the statement added.
Israel has occupied the West Bank since 1967. More than 500,000 Israeli settlers live in the territory, excluding east Jerusalem, among some three million Palestinians.
The United Nations recently warned that settler violence in the West Bank has reached record levels, with an average of six attacks daily causing casualties or damage.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lime plans to name Uber as anchor investor in IPO
Article:
NEW YORK: Lime, which operates an electric bike and scooter network, plans to name ride-hailing firm Uber as an anchor investor in its US initial public offering, The Information reported on Sunday, citing a source.
Uber is expected to invest a "meaningful" amount in the deal and be named as an anchor investor on the cover of an updated IPO prospectus that Lime plans to file on Monday, the report said.
Reuters could not immediately verify the report. Uber and Lime did not immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.
San Francisco-based startup Lime is backed by Uber Technologies. It filed to go public in the United States in May.
Lime intends to use the IPO proceeds to fund operations, repay all its debt, and invest in or acquire complementary technologies, assets or intellectual property.
Uber has guaranteed a US$115 million Lime loan due in September, according to the report.
Separately, Lime plans to start talks with IPO investors on a roadshow this week to raise about US$200 million at a valuation of about US$1.8 billion, the report said.
The startup reported a jump in annual revenue in its filing for the US initial public offering in May.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan finance minister vows readiness to act on yen at any time
Article:
TOKYO: Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Monday that authorities are prepared to respond appropriately to currency moves at any time, repeating the warning as the yen trades near a four-decade low.
"I would refrain from commenting on current foreign exchange situations, but we will respond appropriately to foreign exchange as needed at any time," Katayama said at a regular press conference.
The Japanese yen weakened as far as 161.8 per dollar last week, its lowest level since July 2024, wiping out gains made after a round of interventions from April 30, as a hawkish tilt by the Federal Reserve has led traders to ramp up bets on interest rate increases this year.
A break above the currency pair's 2024 high of 161.96 would send the yen to its weakest level since 1986.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thunderstorm warning issued for Putrajaya, Labuan and 10 states
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Meteorological Department (METMalaysia) has issued a thunderstorm, strong winds and heavy rain warning for the Federal Territories of Putrajaya and Labuan, as well as 10 other states, effective until 9am today.
In a statement, METMalaysia said the affected states include Perlis, Penang and Kedah, specifically Langkawi, Kubang Pasu, Kota Setar, Pokok Sena, Yan, Pendang, Kuala Muda, Kulim and Bandar Baharu.
Apart from Putrajaya and Labuan, areas in Perak (Kerian, Larut, Matang dan Selama, Manjung, Perak Tengah, Bagan Datuk and Hilir Perak) and Selangor (Sabak Bernam, Kuala Selangor, Klang, Petaling, Kuala Langat and Sepang) are also expected to experience heavy rain and strong winds.
Similar weather conditions are forecast for Melaka, Negri Sembilan (Seremban, Port Dickson, Kuala Pilah, Rembau, Jempol and Tampin) and Johor (Tangkak, Muar, Batu Pahat and Pontian).
It added that parts of Sarawak, including Miri and Limbang, as well as several areas in Sabah, will also be affected.
In Sabah, the affected areas in the Interior are Sipitang, Tenom, Kuala Penyu, Beaufort, Keningau and Tambunan.
In the West Coast, the affected areas are Papar, Putatan, Penampang, Kota Kinabalu and Ranau.
Tawau, Sandakan (Tongod) and Kudat (Pitas and Kudat) are also affected.
METMalaysia said warnings are issued when there are indications of thunderstorms with rainfall intensity exceeding 20mm per hour that are imminent or expected to last for more than an hour.
"A thunderstorm warning is a short-term advisory that remains valid for a duration not exceeding six hours for each issuance," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit eases versus greenback as traders monitor Middle East tensions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened lower against the US dollar on Monday, but strengthened against other major currencies amid cautious trading as market players closely watched Middle East geopolitical tensions unfolding.
At 8am, the local note slipped to 4.1350/1445 against the greenback from last Friday's close of 4.1340/1395.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the ringgit is expected to trade cautiously as traders and investors monitor the US-Iran negotiations.
"Thus far, there has been newsflow that millions of barrels of oil continued to flow through the Straits of Hormuz during the weekend. This suggests the risk of a tight supply of crude oil and liquefied natural gas could be easing despite the ongoing tension.
"At the same time, traders will be focusing on the US Federal Reserve's primary inflation gauge, which is the personal consumption expenditures, to be released on Thursday," he said.
Mohd Afzanizam said the ringgit would trade sideways against the greenback amid weak market sentiment.
WTI and Brent crude are trading at US$77.69 per barrel and US$81.60 per barrel, higher by 2.43 per cent and 1.28 per cent, respectively, from the previous close.
At the opening, the local currency strengthened against the Japanese yen to 2.5613/5674 from 2.5636/5671 at Friday's close, rose against the British pound to 5.4619/4745 from 5.4709/4782 previously. It slipped versus the euro to 4.7404/7513 from 4.7376/7439.
The local note was mixed against regional currencies.
It improved against the Singapore dollar to 3.1992/2071 from 3.2019/2064 and rose against the Thai baht to 12.5503/5850 from 12.5856/6074 at last Friday's close.
The ringgit was almost flat versus the Indonesian rupiah at 232.2/232.8 from 232.2/232.5 previously and was unchanged against the Philippine peso at 6.80/6.82 from 6.80/6.81.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Confirmed Ebola cases in DR Congo cross 1,000 as new infections recorded
Article:
KIGALI (Rwanda): The Democratic Republic of Congo's total confirmed Ebola virus cases surged beyond 1,000 Sunday as new infections were recorded, said health authorities.
Confirmed cases of the disease have climbed to 1,003, including 254 deaths, since the outbreak was declared on May 15, with a mortality rate of 25.3 per cent, Anadolu Ajansi reported Health Ministry update showed.
"Despite this progression, response teams continue active investigations, epidemiological surveillance and prevention actions in affected areas," the ministry said.
Health Minister Roger Kamba said response efforts to the virus continue in the provinces of Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu, with an improvement in the number of recoveries but a decrease in the contact follow-up rate.
At least 100 recoveries have been recorded while 365 patients remain in isolation or hospital.
Last week, the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) warned that the current outbreak could be worse than what was witnessed in the 2014-2016 West African epidemic that killed more than 11,000 people in Guinea, Liberia and Sierra Leone if transmission is not brought under control very soon.
But Kamba said community communication efforts, diagnosis and case management continue to be intensified to curb the spread of the epidemic.
Scientists from the Ugandan Health Ministry's Department of National Health Laboratory and Diagnostic Services and Congo's Institute of National Biomedical Research said last week that the current strain, Ebola Bundibugyo, came from a new wildlife spillover, ruling out the possibility that it could be connected to an old outbreak that lay hidden or continued to spread undetected. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump vows immediate fix as algae, peeling paint plague Reflecting Pool project
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said on Sunday work would begin "immediately" on fixing his beleaguered renovation project of the Reflecting Pool in Washington, which has been blighted with algae and peeling paint.
Trump has championed a makeover for the 610-meter-long pool, bringing in contractors to drain it and paint it "American flag blue."
But not long after the job was complete, the paint began to visibly peel, and the gleaming waters promptly refilled with green pond scum.
"Work will begin immediately on fixing the seriously vandalised Reflecting Pool," Trump wrote Sunday on his Truth Social platform.
"I just inspected it, and could only say to myself, and those gathered around me, WOW, who would do such a thing?" he continued.
As workers scrambled to pump out murky water and dumped hydrogen peroxide into the pool, Trump took no responsibility for what many observers chalked up to shoddy workmanship.
"We met with contractors today, will probably be forced to release and drain much of the water in order to do the necessary repairs, but will have them done as quickly as possible," Trump wrote Saturday on Truth Social.
It's unclear how much that may add to the US$14 million cost reported by US media thus far.
In a lengthy social media post Saturday, the 80-year-old Republican claimed renovations had "worked perfectly" before "disgraceful vandalism," accusing unnamed saboteurs of taking "some form of knife or blade" to the pool to "put a 250-foot long gash" into it, and pouring "corrosive and destructive chemicals into the pool."
Trump has offered no evidence for his latest claim on the renovation woes plaguing the pool.
- OLYMPIAN OR VANDAL -
One person accused of being a Reflecting Pool vandal is former Olympian David Hearn, who told The Washington Post he was on a 84km bike ride when he stopped at the Lincoln Memorial to see the Reflecting Pool renovations for himself.
After he leaned down to touch part of the detached blue paint, the 67-year-old was surrounded by two members of the National Guard and arrested by US Park Police officers on a misdemeanour charge of destruction of government property, the Post reported.
"I didn't destroy or break or peel anything. By the time I realised what was going on, I was being put in handcuffs," Hearn told the newspaper.
He added, "I reached in there, and I was able to grab the end of that flapping piece, the already peeling piece. It was still attached to the bottom. I didn't remove anything."
The National Park Service did not respond to an AFP query seeking comment and confirmation.
An AFP photographer on Friday saw that bits of blue paint were being pumped out of the pool by workers, along with green algae that has blossomed since it was recently refilled, turning the water a swampy colour.
In one place, someone had traced the word "TRUMP" into the green scum on the bottom of the pool, where the blue paint is patchy.
- IN BLOOM -
Trump, who got his professional start in his father's real estate development business, has zealously undertaken several renovations during his second term, including tearing down the east wing of the White House for a ballroom.
The Reflecting Pool is hallowed Washington real estate: it was from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial that civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr. gave his famed "I Have a Dream" speech in 1963 to several hundred thousand people gathered around the pool.
The renovation is among the preparations for the 250th anniversary celebrations of independence from in the United States this summer, on July 4.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australian police seize record 2.7 tonnes of cocaine
Article:
SYDNEY: Australian police seized a record 2.7 tonnes of cocaine hidden in plastic tubs buried underground on the outskirts of Sydney, detectives said Monday.
It was the largest cocaine haul in Australian history, a joint organised crime investigation force said in a statement.
Police allege a Sydney-based organised crime group arranged for a foreign vessel to offload the cocaine in northern Queensland before moving it to Sydney for distribution.
"Investigations into the origin of the drugs remain ongoing, and we will work with our international and domestic law enforcement partners to identify the criminal syndicates,"  Australian Federal Police Commander Stephen Jay said.
Police discovered the cocaine on Friday in plastic tubs buried in underground bunkers that were concealed by false floors, police said.
It was concealed at the back of a property in Londonderry, a semi-rural northwestern suburb of greater Sydney.
Officers arrested two men aged 21 and 25 who allegedly tried to run away.
Each of the men was charged with possessing a commercial quantity of an unlawfully imported drug – an offence that carries a maximum penalty of life imprisonment.
Six others allegedly involved had already been arrested and charged with offences related to illicit drugs possession, police said.
Cashed-up Australians are willing to pay some of the highest prices in the world for drugs such as cocaine and methamphetamine.
The black market has increasingly drawn the attention of sophisticated criminal networks from Southeast Asia to South America.
Cocaine-related deaths in Australia surged 28 percent to a record 141 in 2024, according to an annual report on overdoses released this month by the Penington Institute, which researches drug use.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Why Uncertainty Has Become the New Currency of Power
Article:
ROUGHLY one-fifth of global oil consumption passes through the Strait of Hormuz, making it one of the world's most important maritime chokepoints.
Yet the lessons from the 2026 Hormuz crisis extend far beyond energy markets. Even as diplomatic efforts gradually reduced tensions and shipping traffic resumed, commercial confidence remained fragile.
War-risk insurance premiums stayed elevated, shipping schedules remained disrupted, and businesses continued to prepare for further uncertainty.
For much of modern history, strategic competition revolved around the control of territory, resources and sea lanes.
From the British Empire's command of maritime trade routes to the Cold War's military alliances, power was often measured by the ability to physically control space.
Today, however, influence increasingly derives from the ability to shape expectations.  This reveals an important truth about the contemporary international system.
The defining feature of the twenty-first century may not be the return of great power rivalry, nor even the resurgence of geopolitical competition. Rather, it is the monetisation of uncertainty.
In the 20th century, states projected power primarily through military force, territorial control and economic size. Aircraft carriers, armies and strategic bases represented the most visible symbols of national power.
Today, however, power increasingly manifests itself through the ability to alter risk calculations. Governments, investors, insurers, shipping firms and multinational corporations are all responding to a world where uncertainty itself has become a strategic variable.
The Strait of Hormuz illustrates this reality. The waterway was never completely closed. Global trade did not collapse. Yet the mere possibility of disruption was sufficient to reshape commercial behaviour.
Energy traders adjusted positions. Insurers reassessed risk exposure. Shipping companies altered routes and schedules. Markets responded not only to what had happened, but to what might happen.
The lesson is clear. Geography matters, but risk matters even more
A shipping lane does not need to be physically closed to become economically disruptive. A port does not need to be attacked to become commercially unattractive. A supply chain does not need to be severed to become vulnerable.
All that is required is sufficient uncertainty.
How Insurance, Shipping and Finance Became Strategic Weapons
For centuries, geopolitical influence was measured in territory, military strength and resource control. Increasingly, however, influence is exercised through expectations.
When uncertainty rises beyond a certain threshold, investors postpone decisions, insurers increase premiums, financiers demand higher returns, and corporations begin searching for alternative suppliers and routes
Economic behaviour changes long before any formal disruption occurs.
This is the essence of what might be called the geopolitical economy of risk.
Governments increasingly understand that uncertainty itself possesses economic value. Strategic ambiguity, export controls, sanctions, regulatory barriers and technological restrictions can shape commercial behaviour without requiring direct military confrontation.
The objective is often not coercion through force, but influence through uncertainty.
In many respects, this represents a more sophisticated and less visible form of statecraft.
The disruptions in the Red Sea over the past two years provide an important example.
What began as a regional security challenge quickly evolved into a global economic issue.
Shipping companies rerouted vessels around the Cape of Good Hope. Transit times lengthened. Freight costs increased. Insurance premiums rose. Supply chain management became more complicated and expensive.
The consequences extended far beyond the region itself. Food prices, manufacturing costs and inventory management practices were affected across multiple continents.
A localised security issue became a global inflationary concern.
For trading nations such as Malaysia, the implications are immediate. Nearly every aspect of modern economic life - from energy imports and food supplies to electronics exports and industrial production - depends upon stable global logistics networks.
When geopolitical tensions disrupt shipping routes or increase commercial uncertainty, the effects are eventually transmitted through supply chains, production costs and consumer prices.
The same pattern is increasingly visible in the strategic competition between the United States and China.
Washington has imposed progressively tighter controls on advanced semiconductors, artificial intelligence systems and critical technologies.
Beijing has responded through restrictions on rare earth exports, magnets and other critical minerals essential to modern manufacturing and defence industries.
These measures are often described as trade disputes.
In reality, they represent a broader contest over economic leverage and technological advantage.
Their significance lies not merely in the restrictions themselves, but in the uncertainty they create. Firms must reassess investment decisions. Supply chains must be redesigned. Long-term planning becomes more complicated.
The result is the emergence of an international system in which uncertainty itself has become a strategic asset.
Financial markets react immediately to geopolitical developments.
Insurance firms revise risk assessments. Multinational corporations diversify production networks. Manufacturers seek alternative suppliers.
Every increase in uncertainty carries a cost.
Eventually, those costs appear in freight rates, financing expenses, inflation statistics and consumer prices.
The relationship between geopolitics and economics has therefore become more direct than at any point in recent decades.
What This Means for Malaysia
For Malaysia, these developments carry profound implications.
As one of the world's most trade-dependent economies, Malaysia cannot insulate itself entirely from geopolitical turbulence. The country sits astride some of the world's most important maritime routes while simultaneously participating in highly integrated global manufacturing networks.
Malaysia therefore has a direct interest in preserving an open, stable and rulesbased international trading system.
Yet policymakers must also recognise that volatility is no longer an occasional disruption. It is increasingly becoming a structural feature of the global economy.
The challenge is not simply to manage crises as they emerge. The challenge is to build resilience before they occur.This requires sustained investments in logistics infrastructure, strategic stockpiles, energy security, food security and industrial diversification.
It requires strengthening domestic capabilities while remaining deeply integrated with global markets. It requires balancing efficiency with resilience.
For much of the past three decades, globalisation rewarded countries and companies that minimised costs. Supply chains were optimised for efficiency. Inventories were reduced. Production networks stretched across continents.
The emerging geopolitical environment is changing those assumptions.
Increasingly, the international economy is rewarding those who can absorb shocks, adapt quickly and maintain continuity under conditions of uncertainty.
Resilience is becoming an economic asset.
This shift carries important lessons for policymakers. The objective should not be to predict every geopolitical crisis. Such a task is impossible. Nor should governments seek to eliminate uncertainty altogether.
Instead, the goal should be to ensure that institutions, infrastructure and supply chains remain functional when uncertainty inevitably arises.
The world is entering an era where risk itself has become a currency of power. Nations that understand this reality will be better positioned to navigate an increasingly complex international environment.
The great strategic competition of our era may not be fought over territory alone, but over who can best manage, absorb and ultimately price uncertainty.
In the decades ahead, the most successful nations may not be the most powerful militarily, nor even the largest economically. They may simply be the most resilient.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Insurance premiums may remain high despite Hormuz reopening
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: War-risk insurance premiums are expected to stay high even after the Strait of Hormuz reopens, according to maritime analyst and scholar Nazery Khalid.
The rebound in Gulf shipping could be slow as insurers and shipowners remain wary over whether the US-Iran peace deal will hold following June 15 truce.
Marine underwriters are likely to stay cautious, viewing the agreement as a fragile truce amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty, Nazery added.
"Marine underwriters will take a cautiously optimistic view of the situation despite the announcement of a peace deal which seems like a fragile truce," he told Business Times.
The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of global oil supply and a significant share of seaborne LNG, and the insurance disruption has fed directly into energy flows.
According to hormuzstraitmonitor.com, standard marine hull and cargo insurance does not cover risks such as war, terrorism, mines or military action.
To cover these risks, shipowners must buy separate war-risk insurance, which is usually charged as a percentage of a vessel's insured value.
A modern very large crude carrier or VLCC is typically worth about US$100 million-US$150 million. At a normal war-risk premium of around 0.15 per cent, insurance costs roughly US$150,000-US$225,000 per voyage.
But during periods of conflict or crisis, premiums can spike sharply. At about five per cent, the cost can jump to US$5 million-US$7.5 million for a single transit.
Nazery noted that several issues remain unresolved. This includes Iran's nuclear programme, demands related to regional hostilities and questions surrounding the implementation of the agreement, factors that could continue to influence insurers' assessment of risks in the region.
As a result, it would be premature to expect freight rates and other shipping-related costs to quickly return to pre-conflict levels.
"As much as shipowners welcome the cessation of hostilities on the ground, tension still prevails and the possibility of the conflict resuming cannot be ruled out," he said.
Shipowners, he added, are likely to closely monitor developments before fully restoring vessel deployments through the Gulf.
"My guess is they will continue to monitor the situation very closely and make decisions to sail their ships out of the Strait of Hormuz and release capacity to carry cargo again slowly and in a very cautious manner."
Nazery noted that shipping companies would only resume regular transit through the strait when they are confident that the safety of vessels and crews can be guaranteed, attacks on commercial ships have ceased, insurance premiums have eased and the waterway's war-risk status has been revoked.
Beyond insurance concerns, the industry also faces the challenge of clearing a substantial backlog of vessels that accumulated during the disruption.
"It would be anybody's guess how long it would take to clear the massive backlog of ships that had built up in the Strait of Hormuz since it was blockaded at the outset of the conflict," he said.
Drawing a comparison with the supply chain disruptions experienced during the Covid-19 pandemic, Nazery said it took up to six months for severe bottlenecks to ease, suggesting that the recovery process in the Gulf shipping market could also take time.
He said the tanker segment has been the most affected by the closure of the strait, which facilitates nearly 20 per cent of global seaborne energy trade.
Given the volume of vessels and cargo affected, clearing the backlog could take several months.
Nazery said meaningful and lasting improvements in security conditions would require attacks on ships and seafarers to cease completely, strict adherence to the terms of the peace agreement and a comprehensive end to hostilities among the parties involved.
Until then, he expects shipowners and insurers to remain cautious, with the pace of recovery in Gulf shipping activity dependent on improvements in security conditions and confidence that the agreement will hold.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tabung Haji sharpens shift to customer-centric financial institution
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Lembaga Tabung Haji is stepping up efforts to transform itself from being perceived largely as a haj management and travel facilitator into a customer-centric Islamic savings and financial institution.
Tabung Haji chairman Tan Sri Abdul Rashid Hussain said the shift forms part of a broader strategy to enhance its long-term sustainability, strengthen its financial position and foster deeper relationships with depositors.
The transformation agenda is aimed at reinforcing the fund's role as a premier Islamic savings institution and ensuring it remains relevant to future generations of depositors.
"We need to shift the mindset. People think we are a travel agent. We facilitate haj, but fundamentally we are a financial institution," he said in an interview.
Abdul Rashid said the transformation is being driven through initiatives focused on improving customer service, strengthening operational efficiency, enhancing digital capabilities and encouraging Malaysians to save consistently for their pilgrimage from an early age.
He noted that Tabung Haji has already undertaken a series of changes, including improvements to its branding, service delivery and customer engagement efforts, to strengthen public confidence in the institution.
"Our ambition is for Malaysians to treat Tabung Haji as their premier savings institution. We want them to save regularly with us, build their financial capability and be fully prepared when their turn to perform haj arrives."
As at May 31 this year, Tabung Haji's total assets stood at RM110.1 billion, while assets under management (AUM) amounted to RM97.3 billion and total fund size reached RM95.26 billion.
According to him, Tabung Haji currently has 9.7 million depositors, of whom about four million are registered for haj.
More than 55 per cent of depositors have savings below RM15,000, reflecting the need to encourage stronger and more disciplined savings habits.
To address this, Tabung Haji is preparing a new savings-focused initiative designed to encourage Malaysians to start saving for haj from a young age and contribute regularly through structured financial planning.
Abdul Rashid said many prospective pilgrims continue to decline haj offers due to insufficient savings despite having waited years for their turn, highlighting the importance of early financial preparation.
"Every time we offer people the opportunity to perform haj, a significant number reject the offer because they do not have enough money. We need to change that culture and encourage consistent savings from an early age."
Beyond encouraging savings, Tabung Haji is also pursuing greater digitalisation as part of its transformation efforts.
This includes upgrading its digital platforms, enhancing customer-facing applications and modernising internal processes to improve efficiency and service delivery.
Abdul Rashid said the institution's efforts to improve service standards have already received international recognition.
Since 2022, Tabung Haji garnered accolades from Saudi authorities for its management of Malaysian pilgrims, including the prestigious Diamond Award, reflecting the quality of services provided to haj pilgrims.
Abdul Rashid added that the transformation blueprint has largely been completed and is awaiting the necessary approvals before several new initiatives are rolled out.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mobility more important than new highways, says ALR CEO
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Amanat Lebuhraya Rakyat Bhd (ALR) group chief executive officer Muhammad Nizam Alias believes the future of Malaysian travel lies not merely in building highways, but in rethinking mobility itself.
"We foresee that in the future, mobility is  more important than building highways. It's not about connecting point A to point B alone but the whole travel  experience.
"Mobility is about the whole journey. The safety aspect, travel time and experience. It's not so much of just connecting dots. I see mobility as the way of the future," he told the
New Straits Times
.
ALR is a private entity established to take over highway concessions from private toll operators. It currently operates four mature urban highways in the Klang Valley.
Nizam, who was formerly Prasarana Malaysia Bhd president and group chief executive officer, said mobility could extend beyond highways, involving greater integration with public transit.
"We have insufficient public transport services, particularly the  Klang Valley. We have many highways. The initial intention of highways was to address congestion issue but now highways have become congested."
Nizam said mobility reform should consider optimisation of park-and-ride facilities along major highway corridors together with stronger coordination between highways and public transport networks.
"Encouraging commuters to shift modes before entering high density corridors can significantly improve traffic distribution and reduce peak hour pressure within city centres. Highways cannot operate in isolation."
He added that in the future, highway operators would need to function more as system coordinators.
"Our role must evolve from managing infrastructure to helping optimise how the entire mobility network performs."
He pointed to studies that indicated Kuala Lumpur was losing about RM20 billion annually due to congestion, calling it a serious matter that needed to be given more emphasis.
Nizam said future mobility reform must be backed by stronger digital mobility architecture, including the eventual adoption of multi-lane fast flow (MLFF) systems, expanded number plate recognition capabilities and radio frequency identification integration.
"These technologies form part of the longer-term mobility framework being evaluated to improve traffic efficiency, strengthen enforcement capability and enable better demand management."
On toll fairness, he said:  "Is it fair for people to pay the same price during congestion and also during non-congestion hours? It is not fair. So if we talk about mobility, we need to ensure fairness."
He said congestion pricing had been explored by Kuala Lumpur City Hall and other agencies, adding that many global cities had implemented the measure successfully.
Meanwhile, Nizam clarified that while ALR was operating profitably, returns were not the main focus.
He said ALR's ability to forgo toll hikes and compensation claims stemmed from its dividend-free structure.
Since taking over four highways, ALR has maintained a toll freeze, enabling road users to save about RM400 million annually while reducing government compensation obligations through disciplined financial management.
The idea for ALR, he said, began in 2018 when the government sought to abolish tolls.
He added that the idea was noble  as it aimed to ease the burden faced by the people while providing certainty over the concession period.
Nizam said ALR stepped in as a private entity to help realise the aspiration of ending toll concessions while saving the government close to half a billion ringgit a year in potential compensation for the four highways.
"We want to expedite the return of all these public assets to the government and   people because public infrastructure should be owned by the government and  people."
He projected that by October 2033, ALR's  four highways could be returned to the government, which would then decide whether to maintain minimal tolls or abolish them entirely.
Looking ahead, Nizam said governance would be crucial if a model similar to ALR was adopted elsewhere.
"The government has the ability to raise fund more cheaply than a private enterprise. But it has to look at its fiscal position and… governance.
"If the right governance and reward structures are  not put into place, we will not be able to deliver a good infrastructure to the people," he added.
Beyond financial restructuring, ALR is piloting behavioural interventions to ease congestion.
"We want to explore the  behavioural change approach," he said, describing plans to implement "zip merging" at road junctions.
"Congestion is easy to resolve but the challenge is to change the behaviour of the public."
To enforce road discipline, he said ALR would adopt a carrot-and-stick approach with clearer lane demarcations and enforcement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nanta: Multi-lane fast flow rollout must not hit motorists' pockets
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government will not interfere in  talks  between highway concessionaires over the implementation of multi-lane fast flow (MLFF) tolling  system but it comes with one firm condition: Road users must not be made to pay more.
Works Minister Datuk Seri Alexander Nanta Linggi said  business negotiations among stakeholders were in their advanced stages, though a final agreement had yet to be reached.
He said aligning 34 concessionaires  was more difficult compared to countries where a single body controlled the entire highway network.
"It is a business-to-business  model, meaning there is very little government intervention or participation in that.
"We allow people who are in this business to get together, to work things out, and come out with the best system and for the best pricing," he told the
New Straits Times
.
He said pricing was a central concern.
"This is important because the system can be very good, but if we have to charge higher toll, then it's no good for us because we do not want our road users to be burdened with higher cost."
Nanta  said the goal was a single, seamless system that road users could navigate without confusion, regardless of which concessionaire operated the stretch of highway they travel on.
"What we want is for road users to not be burdened by a fragmented system.
"They must have one complete system, fully integrated, so that road users can use it seamlessly.
"But it's not easy for us to tell them to adopt one system.  They have to agree among themselves," he said, adding that not all concessionaires would necessarily come on board, as some highway alignments might not require the system.
Nanta  said Malaysia was taking a gradual, safety-first approach to autonomous vehicles, building the necessary regulatory and infrastructure foundations before any large-scale deployment.
"We must have regulations in place. On the regulatory side, focus areas include safety standards, legal liability, cyber security and data governance.
"On infrastructure readiness, improvements are ongoing in road markings, signage, road safety standards and digital connectivity. or as some people call it 'road furniture'.
"We don't want to simply jump into it. We want to take it slow and steady to ensure that the safety aspect is not compromised."
He pointed to Kuching's Autonomous Rail Transit  project as an example of the direction of travel system, highlighting that dedicated lanes and infrastructure were already being built  in Sarawak's capital.
On the use of drones and artificial intelligence (AI) for road maintenance, Nanta  said deployment had begun at selective operational levels, with drones being used to inspect bridges, slopes and difficult road sections.
He said high-speed weigh-in-motion systems had been installed at 11 federal road locations to detect overloaded vehicles before they could damage road surfaces.
Nanta  said the ministry was pressing ahead with the National Intelligent Traffic Management Centre, which would function as a centralised hub integrating real-time traffic data from the Malaysian Highway Authority, local authorities such as Kuala Lumpur City Hall, the Road Transport Department and other enforcement agencies and highway concessionaires.
He said the centre would use big data analytics and AI to support congestion forecasting, route optimisation and adaptive traffic coordination, including anticipating traffic surges during festive periods, such as Hari Raya.
